title: Dog Retained Deciduous Teeth: A Guide to Baby Teeth Issues
meta_desc: Find out what to do if your puppy has retained baby teeth. Learn why this low-urgency issue needs vet attention and how a photo helps with triage.
content_html:
What are Retained Deciduous Teeth?

Retained deciduous teeth occur when a puppy's baby teeth fail to fall out to make room for the permanent adult teeth. This often results in a "double row" of teeth, most commonly seen with the upper canine teeth. It is a common developmental milestone issue where the adult tooth root does not properly trigger the absorption of the baby tooth root.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. Retained baby teeth are not an immediate life-threatening emergency. However, they should not be ignored. If left in place, these extra teeth can cause dental crowding, trap food and debris, and lead to premature periodontal disease or a painful misaligned bite. You should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to discuss extraction.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Your Vet

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your dog’s gum line is incredibly helpful for triage. It allows a veterinary professional to see the position of the adult tooth relative to the baby tooth and check for signs of gum inflammation or infection. High-quality images help us determine if the issue needs to be addressed during your next scheduled visit or if a separate dental consultation is required sooner.

faqs.question: Can I pull my dog's retained baby tooth at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pull the tooth yourself. Puppy tooth roots are very long and fragile; if they break off during an amateur extraction, it can cause a deep infection or damage the developing adult tooth.

faqs.question: When do puppies usually lose their baby teeth?
faqs.answer: Most puppies lose their deciduous teeth between 4 and 7 months of age. If you still see baby teeth alongside adult teeth after 6 or 7 months, it is time to consult a vet.

faqs.question: How are retained teeth usually treated?
faqs.answer: The standard treatment is surgical extraction by a veterinarian. This is often done at the same time as a spay or neuter procedure to avoid putting the dog under anesthesia twice.
